cover.title: THE MOUNTAIN
cover.subtitle: The one who holds everyone up, and rarely lets anyone hold them.
cover.tagline: You carry so much alone that people forget you need somewhere to lean too.

profile_scores.caption: High Stability · High Care, Low Expression — Always holding others up, rarely leaning on anyone or showing it

short_summary: You love by becoming someone others can lean on — steady, quiet, and hard to shake.
The loneliness you've felt before wasn't because you were alone. It was because you were always the one holding on.
What you're still learning isn't how to be stronger. It's how to let someone support you too.

full_report.headline: THE MOUNTAIN
full_report.intro: You love by becoming steady for the people you care about, often before they even ask. This report follows why you keep becoming the one who holds everything together, what that costs you, and what changes when care finally goes both ways.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're not someone who leans on others easily.
When something hard happens, you handle it yourself first. When a problem comes up, you think it through alone before saying a word. You try not to bother people if you can help it.
So people think you're strong. They think you're dependable. They think you're the mature one.
But here's the thing — that's only half true. You need someone too. You've just forgotten how to say it, for a long time now.
It's the same when you love someone. When they're struggling, you stay close. When they're shaking, you hold them steady. When they're falling apart, you catch them.
But when you're the one who's shaking — you say nothing.
So this happens: people have no idea how much you're actually carrying.
chapters.profile_note: High Stability · High Care, Low Expression — Always holding others up, rarely leaning on anyone or showing it

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: This pattern is probably older than you think.
Someone struggles. You're naturally there beside them. They never even asked. It just happens. And slowly, they get more comfortable — while you get more used to it.
That's where the problem starts. They start to think you're "fine." But you were just acting fine. That gap is huge.
They think you don't need anything. You never say that you do.
So the longer the relationship goes on, the lonelier you get.
chapters.highlight: You're not lonely because you're alone. You're lonely because you've only ever played the role of the one who holds on.

chart.title: THE UNSEEN WEIGHT CURVE
chart.description: As the relationship goes on, responsibility keeps growing — while asking for help keeps shrinking.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I'm just someone who's comfortable being alone.
items.truth:
Hmm. Probably not.

It's not that being alone is comfortable. It's that being alone is *familiar*. Familiar and comfortable aren't the same thing.
items.truth_short: Being alone may feel familiar, but familiar and comfortable aren't the same thing.

items.belief: My problems are mine to solve.
items.truth:
Why? Who decided that?

Think about it — if someone you cared about told you they were struggling, you'd help them. So why are you the exception?
items.truth_short: You would help someone you cared about. You don't have to be the exception.

items.belief: Showing that I'm struggling is a burden to others.
items.truth:
No, it isn't.

The burden isn't asking for help. The burden is never giving the other person a chance to show up for you in the first place. If someone's going to care about you, they at least need to know how you're actually doing.
items.truth_short: Asking for help isn't the burden — never letting someone show up for you is.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You're drawn, again and again, to people who clearly need something to lean on. Someone a little unsteady. Someone a little shaky. Someone who wants to lean on you.
Because you're good at that role. Taking them in, listening, holding steady.
But over time, something gets strange. They're receiving help from you — and you're not receiving help from anyone.
chapters.highlight: Someone who trusts you uses your shoulder. Someone who loves you also checks whether that shoulder is getting heavy.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to get even stronger. You're already strong enough.
What you need is the opposite. A little weaker. More precisely — to show the weaker parts.
Say it when something's hard. Ask for things. Let yourself receive help.
It takes more courage than it sounds like it should.
But that's what actually changes a relationship.
chapters.action_sentence: But that's what actually changes a relationship.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to take responsibility. You already know how to protect someone. You already know how to stay until the end.
What you're going to learn next is how to hold on together.
Not holding on alone — but letting the other person be part of holding on too.
The best relationships, it turns out, are carried by two people at once.
chapters.transition_note: The best relationships, it turns out, are carried by two people at once.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: The Person Who Looks Strong But Needs Someone
items.description: They seem capable. They take care of things. People rely on them. But when no one is watching, they need someone to hold everything together for them.

items.type_title: The Person Who Needs Somewhere To Lean
items.description: Something goes wrong, and you're the first person they call. A difficult day. A family problem. A decision they don't want to make alone. At first, being needed feels meaningful. But slowly, you realize they only know how to stand when you're holding part of the weight.

items.type_title: Someone who can't quite manage on their own
items.description: They struggle to decide things. They struggle to keep things organized. So you end up doing it. At some point, far more of it than you should.

items.type_title: Someone Who Says Thank You But Never Changes
items.description: They appreciate you. They mean it. But appreciation alone doesn't make a relationship healthier. You keep giving. They keep thanking you. And nothing actually changes.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs were there every time. You just kept explaining them away — on the other person's behalf.

signal_strength.label: You hear about their problems often, but you barely mention your own
They ask you for help far more often than you ask them
Their problems have a place on your schedule, but no one knows about yours
The harder things get, the quieter you become

chapters.core_signal: They believe you're fine. But the only evidence for that belief is a performance you've been putting on.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: Asking for help isn't strange.
checklist.description: You don't have to read the room. You don't have to feel sorry about it. You just say it.

checklist.title: You don't have to be strong all the time.
checklist.description: Some days you can be exhausted. You can make mistakes. You can lean on someone.

checklist.title: When problems come up, you move on it together.
checklist.description: One person doesn't become the fixer. You look at it together. You think it through together.

checklist.title: The care goes both ways.
checklist.description: This is the most important one. You're not the only one watching the other person. They're watching you too.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you:
You're vulnerable to someone who isn't able to solve their own problems.

At first it looks fine. Helping them feels a little like love. Being needed feels meaningful.

But eventually, the same thing happens again. You stop being a partner and become a fixer.
sections.who_steadies_you:
There's also someone who's a good match for you, in reverse: someone who doesn't carry every problem alone. Someone who says when things are hard. Someone who asks when they need something. Someone who actually says thank you.

This kind of person doesn't use up your sense of responsibility. They share it with you.
sections.next_signs:
This time it'll show up sooner.

In how quickly they tell you about their hard days, and in how you listen when it's about yours.

What used to take months to notice, you'll catch within weeks this time.

Who's the one always receiving. Who's actually showing up in the relationship.
sections.try_this:
This time, run a test. One very small favor. Something genuinely minor.

Ask them to come pick you up. Or to give you a call. Anything at all.

Then watch.

Does the favor annoy them? Or do they take it like it's an opportunity?

You've spent your whole life helping others. This time, check something else.

Whether this is someone who can help you too.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to support someone else. What's left is learning to let care move both ways.
action_items: This week, ask for one small favor instead of handling everything yourself.
When something feels hard, say it before you have already solved it alone.
Notice whether the other person checks on your weight, not just uses your shoulder.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to be someone else's support. The next report covers why you keep ending up in the role of the one who holds on — and how you relearn what it means to lean on someone.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
